planting.note: Plant container shrubs in late winter, spring, or fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Magic Carpet's best leaf color — vivid red-orange new growth — develops in full sun; in partial shade the foliage is more yellow-green and less vivid.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Magic Carpet is one of the most spectacular compact spiraea cultivars for foliage color — a dwarf selection staying 12–24 inches tall that produces a dramatic seasonal foliage display beginning with vivid red-orange to scarlet new growth in spring, maturing to yellow-gold through summer, and transitioning to orange-red fall color. The three-season foliage interest — red spring, gold summer, orange fall — combined with pink summer flowers creates year-round ornamental value rarely matched by such a compact shrub. The flower heads are pink, produced in early summer above the colorful foliage, but the foliage is the primary ornamental feature. Magic Carpet's extremely compact size makes it outstanding for edging, mass planting as a colorful ground cover, container gardens, and foreground positions in shrub borders where larger spiraea would overwhelm. Like all Japanese spiraea cultivars, it is extremely tough and adaptable — tolerating drought, clay soil, urban conditions, and the heat of zone 9 with minimal care. Shear lightly after flowering and again in late summer to encourage fresh, colorful new growth. In zone 9, Magic Carpet tends to be slightly more compact than in cooler zones and performs best in full sun with good drainage. Deer-resistant once established.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in late winter, spring, or fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Little Princess blooms most prolifically in full sun — at least 6 hours of direct sun is required for the best flower display.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Little Princess is a classic compact Japanese spiraea — a neat, mounding dwarf shrub staying 18–30 inches tall with a wider spread, producing masses of small, rose-pink flat-topped flower clusters (corymbs) in late spring to early summer against mid-green foliage. Where Magic Carpet spiraea is grown primarily for foliage color, Little Princess is grown primarily for its prolific pink summer flower display — the small, densely packed flower heads cover the plant in a vivid pink display for 4–6 weeks and attract bees and butterflies intensively during bloom. The compact, mounding habit and fine-textured blue-green to medium-green summer foliage that transitions to orange-red in fall provide seasonal interest beyond the spring bloom. Shear lightly immediately after flowering to promote a second, lighter summer flush and maintain the naturally compact mounding form — cutting back by one-third after spring bloom encourages fresh new growth and often produces additional flowering in late summer. Little Princess is among the most cold-hardy Japanese spiraea cultivars, and its compact size and adaptability to clay and average soils make it one of the most practical and reliable deciduous flowering shrubs for zone 4–8 landscapes.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to alkaline, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun; Moonshine requires excellent drainage — it will not survive in heavy clay or wet soil, particularly during summer humidity in zone 9.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun; Moonshine is the standard hybrid yellow yarrow cultivar — a cross between Achillea clypeolata and A. taygetea — producing large, flat-topped clusters of rich sulfur-yellow flowers from late spring through midsummer above finely cut, aromatic, silver-gray to gray-green, feathery foliage. The cool, muted sulfur-yellow flower color is softer and more refined than the golden-yellow of species yarrow, making Moonshine a superb mixer in the perennial border — it harmonizes with blue flowers (salvias, catmints), orange and coral tones, and white without the dominating brightness of brighter yellows. The silver-gray foliage is attractive even when the plant is not in flower. Moonshine was named the Perennial Plant of the Year in 1990, recognizing its outstanding landscape performance. Like all yarrows, it is extraordinarily drought-tolerant and heat-tolerant — performing best in lean, dry soils where many perennials struggle. The key limitation in zone 9 is summer humidity: yarrow is susceptible to crown rot and foliar diseases in hot, humid, wet conditions. In zone 9, excellent drainage and lean soil are essential; amended sandy or gravelly soils work better than clay. The dried flower heads are excellent for arrangements. Divide every 2–3 years to maintain vigor.
sources: Perennial Plant Association — Perennial Plant of the Year 1990

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; like all yarrows, Terra Cotta requires excellent drainage and lean soil — organic-rich, moist soil promotes soft growth, disease, and short plant life.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Terra Cotta is an outstanding Achillea millefolium cultivar from the Galaxy series — a hybrid selection producing flat-topped flower clusters in a distinctive, warm terra-cotta to salmon-orange color that is unique among common yarrow cultivars. Most commonly grown yarrows come in yellow or white; Terra Cotta's warm orange-salmon tones extend the yarrow color palette into the sunset range, making it excellent for warm-toned color schemes with bronze ornamental grasses, yellow-gold companions, and dark-leafed perennials. The flower color changes as it ages — opening with deep terra-cotta orange and fading to soft salmon-buff as the flowers mature, creating an interesting multi-tonal effect on a single flower head. The finely cut, feathery, aromatic, medium-green foliage is attractive when not in flower. Terra Cotta blooms from early summer through fall with deadheading, and the dried flower heads hold their warm tones well for dry arrangements. Like Moonshine, it is extremely drought-tolerant and heat-tolerant but requires excellent drainage to persist in the humid Gulf South — it is often treated as an annual in heavy clay or wet zone 9 conditions. Divide every 2–3 years to maintain vigor and prevent the center from dying out.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in late winter or early spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Lynwood Gold requires full sun for maximum flower production — plants in partial shade produce fewer flowers but good foliage.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Lynwood Gold is the most widely planted forsythia cultivar and perhaps the most familiar early-spring flowering shrub in American horticulture — a vigorous, upright-arching shrub that literally covers itself with vivid golden-yellow flowers in late February through March (in zone 8) before any leaves emerge, creating an explosive, golden-yellow color display that signals the end of winter. The flowers are produced on second-year wood along the entire length of the arching stems before any foliage appears — when in full bloom, a mature Lynwood Gold shrub is a mass of pure golden yellow visible from a considerable distance. This spectacle is the quintessential early spring garden moment in zone 5–8. Note: forsythia's flower bud hardiness is its limiting characteristic for zone 9 use — the flower buds require a period of cold dormancy and are damaged by extended warm winters in zone 9, where reliable flowering cannot be counted on every year. In zone 9, forsythia may flower well in cool-winter years and produce sparse bloom in mild winters. For zone 9 plantings, consider Karl Sax and Meadowlark cultivars with enhanced flower bud cold-hardiness, or accept the variable bloom performance of standard cultivars.
